description:
You are a container-platform specialist who has just been asked to run a quick in-container network-diagnostics sweep for two legacy micro-services that ship only as static file trees.  
Both services live in the host filesystem and must be started with a lightweight HTTP server, then tested from the same shell session.  
Your job is to (1) launch the two services, (2) probe them from the container itself, and (3) record the probe results in a strictly-formatted log file that an automated checker will read.

Starting point already present in the container
1. Directory structure  
   • /home/user/microservices/auth  
   • /home/user/microservices/billing  

2. Static content already inside those directories  
   /home/user/microservices/auth/index.html     (contains the string “Hello from auth service”)  
   /home/user/microservices/billing/index.html  (contains the string “Hello from billing service”)

Requirements you must satisfy
A. Launch each directory as a tiny HTTP service that listens only on 127.0.0.1, using distinct ports:
   • auth    → 127.0.0.1:5001  
   • billing → 127.0.0.1:5002

   (Any tool is acceptable—Python’s built-in http.server is fine—as long as the service stays running in the background while you perform the probes.)

B. Verify both services from the current shell with a single HTTP probe per service (for example, curl or wget).  
   Only the root URL “/” needs to be fetched.

C. Create the directory /home/user/diagnostics (if it does not already exist) and inside it create the file /home/user/diagnostics/network_report.log.

   The file must contain exactly three lines in plain-text CSV format, using a comma (,) as field delimiter and **no additional whitespace** anywhere in the file (including at the ends of lines).  
   The required, *precise* contents are:

   service_name,endpoint,status
   auth,http://127.0.0.1:5001,UP
   billing,http://127.0.0.1:5002,UP

   Notes on the required format:
   • The header line must appear exactly as written above.  
   • Use lowercase service names (“auth”, “billing”).  
   • The literal word “UP” (uppercase) must be used for a service that answers successfully with HTTP status 200.  
   • There must be **no trailing newline** after the third line (i.e., the file’s last byte is the final “P” in “UP”).

D. Leave both HTTP services running in the background when you finish.  
   (The automated grader will only inspect the log file, but keeping the servers up ensures your diagnostic commands were truly executed.)

What the grader will check
1. That /home/user/diagnostics/network_report.log exists.  
2. That the file’s contents are **identical**, byte-for-byte, to the three-line CSV shown above.  
3. That the file does not contain superfluous whitespace or extra newlines.

Remember: do not output the raw shell commands here—perform them in the terminal. Take care to ensure the log file formatting is perfect, because even an extra space or newline will cause automatic verification to fail.
truth:
Initial filesystem state that exists **before** the agent starts:

/home/user/microservices/auth/index.html  
  └── “Hello from auth service” (single line, newline-terminated)
/home/user/microservices/billing/index.html  
  └── “Hello from billing service” (single line, newline-terminated)

After successful completion the grader expects the following *exact* file (all other checks are advisory only):

File: /home/user/diagnostics/network_report.log  
Size: 94 bytes  
Hex dump:  
  73 65 72 76 69 63 65 5f 6e 61 6d 65 2c 65 6e 64 70 6f 69 6e 74 2c 73 74 61 74 75 73 0a  
  61 75 74 68 2c 68 74 74 70 3a 2f 2f 31 32 37 2e 30 2e 30 2e 31 3a 35 30 30 31 2c 55 50 0a  
  62 69 6c 6c 69 6e 67 2c 68 74 74 70 3a 2f 2f 31 32 37 2e 30 2e 30 2e 31 3a 35 30 30 32 2c 55 50

ASCII representation (no trailing newline):
service_name,endpoint,status
auth,http://127.0.0.1:5001,UP
billing,http://127.0.0.1:5002,UP
name: task_000000_b3a8033c
